title: How much does it cost to run an electric blanket?
description: An electric blanket costs roughly 2 to 4p an hour to run. Full UK costs per night, per month and per winter at the current price cap, with savings tips.
blurb: Pennies a night, not pounds. We work out exactly what an electric blanket costs to run in the UK this winter.
lede: An electric blanket is one of the cheapest forms of warmth you can buy, yet plenty of people still hesitate before switching one on, half convinced it is quietly inflating the bill. The truth is almost the opposite. A blanket heats the person rather than the room, which means it can deliver real comfort from a wattage smaller than a single old-fashioned light bulb. Once you put the actual numbers against the current price cap, the case is hard to argue with: an evening's preheat costs less than 3p, and even a heavy winter of nightly use struggles to reach £10. Here is the full breakdown, with the working shown so you can swap in your own blanket's wattage and your own tariff.
short_answer: A typical double electric underblanket is rated at about 100 watts, which costs around 2.6p per hour at the July to September 2026 price cap rate of 26.11p per kWh. Preheating the bed for an hour every night through a six-month winter comes to roughly £4.75 in total. Even left on a low setting all night, the thermostat cycles the element on and off, so a full night usually costs somewhere between 5p and 10p.

sections.h2: What an electric blanket actually uses
sections.html:
Electric blankets are low-powered by design. The heating element is a thin wire woven through the fabric, and because it sits directly against the bedding it does not need much power to make a noticeable difference. Typical ratings on the UK market are:

  • Single underblanket: 40 to 60 watts
  • Double underblanket: 60 to 100 watts
  • King-size underblanket: 100 to 150 watts, often split into two independently controlled halves of about 75 watts each
  • Heated throw or overblanket: 100 to 160 watts, as these have to work harder sitting on top of you in a cold room rather than trapped under a duvet

You will not find an official efficiency rating on the box, because electric blankets are not covered by the scheme described in our guide to energy labels. The number that matters is the wattage printed on the rating label, usually found on a small tag near the controller or the plug. That figure is the maximum draw on the highest setting, and as we will see below, the blanket rarely pulls that much for long.

To turn a wattage into a running cost, the sum is the same one we use across our appliance running cost guides: watts divided by 1,000 gives kilowatts, multiplied by hours of use gives kWh, multiplied by your unit rate gives pence. A 100 watt blanket running flat out for one hour uses 0.1 kWh, which at 26.11p per kWh costs 2.6p.

sections.h2: The cost per hour, per night and per winter
sections.html:
The table below shows what each common blanket type costs at full power, plus what a sensible nightly habit adds up to over a week and over a full October to March winter of 182 nights.

Blanket typeTypical wattageCost per hour1 hour a night, per week1 hour a night, full winter
Single underblanket60W1.6p11p£2.85
Double underblanket100W2.6p18p£4.75
King-size underblanket150W3.9p27p£7.13
Heated throw / overblanket160W4.2p29p£7.60

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Winter column assumes 182 nights, October to March, one hour per night at full power.

Those winter totals are worst-case in one important respect: they assume the blanket draws its full rated wattage for the whole hour. In practice the built-in thermostat cuts the element in and out once the blanket reaches temperature, so the true figures will sit a little below the table. Either way, the headline holds. A whole winter of nightly preheating costs less than a single takeaway coffee or two.

If you prefer a monthly figure, a 100 watt double blanket used for an hour every night comes to 3 kWh a month, or about 78p.

sections.h2: Why your real cost is probably even lower
sections.html:
Three things push the real-world cost below the rated-wattage maths.

The thermostat does most of the work

Every modern blanket has a thermostat in the controller. On the high setting it runs the element hard until the blanket warms up, then cycles it on and off to hold temperature. On the lower settings it spends most of its time off. A 100 watt blanket set to low for a full eight-hour night typically averages 25 to 40 watts of actual draw, which is 0.2 to 0.32 kWh, or roughly 5p to 8p for the entire night. Across a whole winter that all-night habit costs around £10 to £15, which is still less than many households spend on a single evening of central heating.

Most people only preheat

The most common pattern is switching the blanket on 20 to 30 minutes before bed and turning it off as you climb in. Half an hour at 100 watts is 0.05 kWh, or about 1.3p a night. Over the full winter that is roughly £2.40. At that level the running cost is genuinely too small to worry about.

Dual controls halve the waste

If you share a king-size bed and only one of you feels the cold, a dual-control blanket lets you heat one half only. That cuts a 150 watt blanket to about 75 watts, and the cost in proportion.

If you want to confirm your own blanket's behaviour rather than trust the label, a cheap plug-in energy monitor between the controller and the socket will show you the real draw, including how often the thermostat is actually firing.

sections.h2: Electric blanket vs heating the room
sections.html:
The comparison that really matters is not the blanket against nothing, it is the blanket against the alternatives people reach for on a cold night.

  • 2kW fan heater: about 52p per hour at the current cap. Running one for an hour before bed costs twenty times what the blanket does, and the heat is gone within minutes of switching it off.
  • Oil-filled radiator on a 1kW setting: about 26p per hour, ten times the blanket.
  • Keeping the central heating on later: heating a whole house with gas for an extra hour typically costs somewhere between 50p and £1.50 depending on the property, against under 3p for the blanket.

The reason the blanket wins so decisively is physics rather than cleverness. A room heater has to warm hundreds of kilograms of air, walls and furniture before you feel much benefit. The blanket warms a few kilograms of bedding that sit directly against your skin, and the duvet then traps that heat all night. Heating the person instead of the space is the single cheapest warmth strategy there is, and the bed is the easiest place to apply it.

We compare these options in proper detail in our guide to the electric blanket versus heating question, and if you do need to warm an actual room, our breakdown of portable heater running costs shows which type costs least for the job.

The running cost is already small, but a few habits trim it further and keep the blanket safe.

  • Preheat, then switch off. Unless you genuinely sleep cold, 30 minutes before bed does the job. Your own body heat plus the duvet maintains the warmth from there.
  • Use the timer if you have one. Many mid-range blankets include an auto-off after one to twelve hours. Set it to an hour and you cannot accidentally leave it running all day, which is the only realistic way an electric blanket ever becomes expensive.
  • Drop the setting before you drop the blanket. If high feels too warm, medium or low costs proportionally less because the thermostat fires less often.
  • Switch off at the wall if the controller stays warm. Modern controllers draw well under a watt on standby, which is pennies a year, so this is more about safety than savings. Our guide to standby power covers which devices are worth unplugging and which are not.
  • Retire old blankets. Electric blankets over ten years old, or any blanket with creased wiring, scorch marks or a frayed cable, should be replaced. Fire services run free checking events in many areas each autumn. A new blanket costs £25 to £60 and, as the table above shows, the electricity to run it is a rounding error by comparison.
  • Never combine it with a hot water bottle, and never use an underblanket folded or on an adjustable bed unless the manufacturer specifically allows it.

An electric blanket costs about 1.6p to 4.2p per hour to run at the current price cap, depending on size. The typical habit of preheating the bed each evening adds £3 to £8 to your bill across an entire winter, and even sleeping with it on low all night only pushes that to £10 to £15. Set against a 52p-per-hour fan heater or an extra hour of central heating every night, it is comfortably the cheapest warmth in the house. If you are cutting back on heating to save money, an electric blanket is one of the first things to buy rather than one of the things to switch off.

faq.q: How much does it cost to leave an electric blanket on all night?
faq.a: Less than most people expect. On a low setting the thermostat cycles the element on and off, so a 100 watt double blanket averages roughly 25 to 40 watts across an eight-hour night. That is 0.2 to 0.32 kWh, or about 5p to 8p per night at the July to September 2026 price cap rate of 26.11p per kWh. Many manufacturers still recommend using only blankets specifically designed for all-night use this way.

faq.q: How much does an electric blanket cost per month?
faq.a: A 100 watt double blanket used for one hour every night uses about 3 kWh a month, which costs roughly 78p at 26.11p per kWh. If you only preheat for half an hour, it is around 39p a month. A heated throw used for three hours every evening costs more, at roughly £3.75 a month.

faq.q: Does an electric blanket use a lot of electricity compared with a heater?
faq.a: No. A typical electric blanket draws 60 to 160 watts, while a fan heater draws 2,000 watts and an oil-filled radiator 1,000 to 2,500 watts. Hour for hour the blanket costs ten to thirty times less, because it warms you directly instead of warming the whole room.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to use an electric blanket or put the heating on?
faq.a: The blanket, by a wide margin, if the goal is a warm bed. An hour of a 100 watt blanket costs about 2.6p, while an extra hour of gas central heating typically costs 50p to £1.50 for a whole house. The heating only makes sense when you need the room itself warm, for example if you will be up and about rather than under a duvet.

faq.q: Do electric blankets use power when switched off at the controller?
faq.a: A tiny amount. Modern controllers draw well under one watt on standby, which works out at pennies over a full year. Switching off at the wall is still good practice for an electric blanket, but the reason is safety rather than any meaningful saving.
